# Lex

Bộ gõ Tiếng Việt dùng kiểu gõ Telex được tùy chỉnh phù hợp với cách dùng của tác giả.

Mục đích của chương trình là để hiểu rõ hơn về tính khả thi của cấu trúc chương trình hỗ trợ đa nền
tảng trong đó phần thuật toán chính sẽ được viết trong 1 thư viện dùng chung bằng Zig và chương
trình chính được viết bởi ngôn ngư phù hợp với từng nền tảng. Ý tưởng này bắt nguồn từ cách lập
trình của
[Dropbox](https://oleb.net/blog/2014/05/how-dropbox-uses-cplusplus-cross-platform-development/) và
[Ghostty](https://mitchellh.com/writing/ghostty-and-useful-zig-patterns).

Lưu ý: Dropbox đã chuyển sang cách lập trình khác vì khó khăn trong lúc tích hợp thư viện, tuyển
người, đào tạo... Những khó khăn này không phải là vấn đề lớn khi tác giả là người duy nhất chịu
trách nhiệm cho toàn bộ chương trình.

## Chạy được trên hệ điều hành

- macOS 26.0 trở về sau.

## Chức năng

- [ ] Nhập Tiếng Việt kiểu Telex với một số tùy chỉnh:
  - [ ] Bỏ dấu kiểu cũ: _òa_, _úy_. Ví dụ: _hòa_, _thúy_.
  - [ ] Không bỏ dấu nếu từ bắt đầu bằng phụ âm không phải Tiếng Việt: _f_, _j_, _w_, _z_.
- [ ] Phím tắt bật / tắt nhập Tiếng Việt: `Ctrl + Opt + Space`.
- [ ] Phím tắt để xóa trạng thái gõ Tiếng Việt: `Ctrl (left)`.
- [ ] Chỉ hiện biểu tượng trên thanh Menu của macOS.
- [ ] Khởi động khi đăng nhập.

## Lộ trình

- [ ] 0.1.x
  - [ ] Chỉ hiện biểu tượng trên thanh Menu của macOS.
  - [ ] Nhập Tiếng Việt kiểu Telex với một số tùy chỉnh:
    - [ ] Bỏ dấu kiểu cũ: _òa_, _úy_. Ví dụ: _hòa_, _thúy_.
    - [ ] Không bỏ dấu nếu từ bắt đầu bằng phụ âm không phải Tiếng Việt: _f_, _j_, _w_, _z_.
  - [ ] Phím tắt bật / tắt nhập Tiếng Việt: `Ctrl + Opt + Space`.
- [ ] 0.2.x
  - [ ] Phím tắt để xóa trạng thái gõ Tiếng Việt: `Ctrl (left)`.
- [ ] 0.3.x
  - [ ] Khởi động khi đăng nhập.
- [ ] 0.4.x
  - [ ] Hoàn thiện cơ chế kiểm thử.
- [ ] 1.0.x - tất cả chức năng đã hoàn thành.

## Tình hình phát triển hiện tại

[Bản mẫu](./playground/prototype-app/) có thể chạy được và gõ được Tiếng Việt. Đang phát triển bản
chính thức. Gần như toàn bộ code Zig, Swift ở bản mẫu là do coding agent viết, code của bản chính
thức sẽ được tác giả tự viết (phần lớn) và dùng coding agent để hỗ trợ những chỗ trùng lặp.

# Lex devlog 1 - 2026-04-22

## Goals

- Define the engine state and test them.
- Understand the technical term, Zig language syntax used in the code.

## Telex knowledge

- Diacritics: dấu của ký tự.
- Tone: dấu thanh.

The name of diacritics and tones were taken from Wikipedia:

- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vietnamese_phonology
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diacritic
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telex_(input_method)

## Zig knowledge

- Must use `extern` to have C ABI guarantee. The `extern` only affect to the current struct, we must
  specify `extern` to all structs that we want C ABI compatible.
- Enum is backed by a tagged type, e.g. `u4` (4 bits unsigned-integer).
- If we want C ABI compatible enum, we must tag it with C compatible type, it could be Zig type,
  just be compatible, e.g. u8 (uint8_t), u16 (uint16_t).
- Found API: https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/std/#std.ascii.isAlphabetic to check alphabet
  character.
- Know how to use `@import`: `const std = @import("std");`.
- `expectEqual` return error union, we must handle error or use `try` to properly throw the error.
- What is `'A'...'Z', 'a'...'z'`?

## Code

- We define nearly good data structure. Now we need to think about initialize, validate the
  structure based on our design: caller allocated memory.
- Use `init` function to create struct instance, include validation in `init`.
- Use `assert` for validation, treat invalid parameters as programming error, non-recoverable.
- Write test near the implementation code.
- Use `init` for the default usage, `init_<more_parameter>` for alternative usage.

## Technical debts

- ~~`inline for`~~.
- ~~`@import`~~.
- ~~Error union? A normal type combine with `!` (error), force the receiving side to handle the
  error.~~

## Tomorrow

- Write `init_diacritic` and tests.

# Lex devlog 2 - 2026-04-23

## Goals

- Write `init_diacritic` and tests.
- Write `init_diacritic_tone` and tests.
- Understand Zig language syntax in new code.

## Development decisions

### Reference documents

- Store reference documents fonud during development in `articles` directory.
- Use PDF format, prefer the PDF provided by the website. Otherwise, use Safari reader mode and
  print as PDF.

### Line width

- Use 100 as consistent line width for code and documents.

## Zig knowledge

- `inline for` is used to know comptime struct.
- Can omit enum name when type is known.
- `switch` can have `else` for remaining cases. We don't need `break` to terminate switch, constrast
  to Java.

## Telex knowledge

- Added a diacritic for `đ`: _bar_.
- All vowels can take all tone.
- List of Vietnamese characters: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet

## Code

- Assertion strategy when dealing with many parameters: use `switch` for main parameter to narrow
  the scope and use `assert` on each arm of the switch. Could use `if` for further narrowing.

# Lex devlog 3 - 2026-04-24

## Goals

- Write `init` for State. The `init` must receive a pointer from allocated memory and initialize the
  State on that buffer.

## Zig knowledge

- The `[16]Span` is a fixed array of Spans. All items must be initialized at the construction.
- Namespaced function such as `State.init` is Zig only term. C has no namespace. We must create a
  wrapper such as `lex_state_init`.
- Need export _size_ and _alignment_ of the struct for memory allocation.

## Telex knowledge

- Non-Vietnamese onset: _f_, _j_, _w_, _z_.

## Code

- To run Swift tests, we must introduce SPM to our code base. Add 2 file: `Package.swift` and
  `macos/LexTests.swift`.

## Technical debts

- ~~Alignment contract? The alignment is a number that determine how many byte is the unit of the
  memory area, the alignment is needed to calculate offset of each field in struct and determine
  where padding should be added. The Wikipedia article about "Data structure alignment" has an
  example about alignment and padding.~~
- ~~Swift SPM? It's package manager:
  https://docs.swift.org/swiftpm/documentation/packagemanagerdocs/~~

# Lex devlog 4 - 2026-04-27

## Goals

- Write some first steps for `lex_add` to understand the function identity.
- Try raw memory allocation and simple tests for `lex_add`.

## Zig knowledge

- `*const State`: read-only pointer. Could not write to the memory.
- `*State`: mutable pointer, could write to the memory. In case of `lex_add`, `*State` is correct.

## Telex knowledge

- At this stage, I want `lex_add` based on the input character to determine the intention, then I
  will have a checklist for each intention.
- Have an overview about possible intentions.

## Tomorrow

- Add assert to `lex_add`.
- Add C header for liblex.
- Add Swift tests to test memory allocation and Swift-Zig interoperate.

./playground/prototype-app/README.md:9:- **Core (`src/liblex.zig`)** — A span-based composition engine. Each keystroke mutates an array of `Span` structs (base letter, modifier, tone, flags). The engine handles modifier application (doubled vowels `aa`→`â`, `w`→`ơ`/`ư`/`ă`, `dd`→`đ`), tone placement following Vietnamese linguistic rules (region analysis: onset → nucleus → coda, with semi-vowel coda detection), cancellation (repeating a filled modifier/tone strips it and enters literal mode), `gi`/`qu` onset binding, and `ươ` auto-fill. Unicode output is produced via lookup tables covering all 12 vowel bases × 6 tone variants in both cases. The engine exposes `add`, `backspace`, `reset`, `get_composed_utf16`, `init_state`, and `state_size`.

# Prototype — Span-based Vietnamese Telex IME

A playground prototype for a Vietnamese Telex input method on macOS. The core engine (`liblex`) is a Zig static library that models each rendered character as a **span** (base + modifier + tone), handling the full Telex input set: all vowels with diacritics (â, ă, ê, ô, ơ, ư), tones (sắc, huyền, hỏi, ngã, nặng), đ, cancellation logic, `gi`/`qu` onset reclassification, and `ươ` auto-fill. The shell (`Lex.swift`) is a macOS menubar app that intercepts keystrokes via `CGEventTap`, feeds them to the engine, and applies a **diff** against the currently rendered text — emitting only the minimal sequence of synthetic backspaces followed by a single multi-character insertion event to update the active application.

## Architecture

The system is split into two layers communicating through a C ABI:

- **Core (`src/liblex.zig`)** — A span-based composition engine. Each keystroke mutates an array of `Span` structs (base letter, modifier, tone, flags). The engine handles modifier application (doubled vowels `aa`→`â`, `w`→`ơ`/`ư`/`ă`, `dd`→`đ`), tone placement following Vietnamese linguistic rules (region analysis: onset → nucleus → coda, with semi-vowel coda detection), cancellation (repeating a filled modifier/tone strips it and enters literal mode), `gi`/`qu` onset binding, and `ươ` auto-fill. Unicode output is produced via lookup tables covering all 12 vowel bases × 6 tone variants in both cases. The engine exposes `add`, `backspace`, `reset`, `get_composed_utf16`, `init_state`, and `state_size`.

- **Shell (`macos/Lex.swift`)** — A menubar-only macOS app (`LSUIElement=true`) that owns the engine state and a rendered-text snapshot. On each keystroke it calls the engine, reads the new composed UTF-16, and diffs it against the snapshot to compute the minimal edit (common-prefix comparison). Replacement characters are emitted in a single multi-character `CGEvent` to reduce flicker. Synthetic events are tagged (`0x4C4558`) to avoid feedback loops. The shell resets engine state on app switches, mouse clicks, modifier keys (Cmd/Ctrl/Alt), navigation keys (arrows, Home/End, Tab, Escape, Return, forward delete), and tap-disabled events. A global hotkey (Ctrl+Opt+Space) toggles Vietnamese input on/off with audio feedback.

- **FFI bridge (`src/liblex.h`)** — C-compatible header declaring the engine API. Swift calls into the Zig static library through this bridging header. The engine is stateless from the caller's perspective — Swift allocates the opaque state buffer (`state_size()` bytes) and passes it to every call.

## Prerequisites

- **Zig ≥ 0.16.0** (master recommended) — [download](https://ziglang.org/download/)
- **Xcode Command Line Tools** — `xcode-select --install`
- **macOS 26** (Tahoe)

## Project Structure

```
prototype-app/
├── src/
│   ├── liblex.zig              # Span-based Telex engine (C library)
│   └── liblex.h                # C bridging header
├── macos/
│   ├── Lex.app/
│   │   └── Contents/
│   │       └── Info.plist      # LSUIElement=true (menubar-only)
│   └── Lex.swift               # macOS app shell (diff-based rendering)
├── build.zig                   # Zig build script (full pipeline)
├── build.zig.zon               # Zig build manifest
└── .tool-versions              # Version management (zig master)
```

## Build

The `build.zig` script automates the entire pipeline — building the Zig static library, re-packing the archive (Zig master workaround for non-8-byte-aligned archive members), compiling Swift, and ad-hoc codesigning — in a single command:

```sh
zig build
```

To build and immediately launch the app:

```sh
zig build run
```

Or run the binary directly to see console output:

```sh
./macos/Lex.app/Contents/MacOS/Lex
```

### Manual steps (for reference)

<details>
<summary>Expand manual build steps</summary>

#### Step 1: Build the Zig static library

```sh
zig build
```

This produces `zig-out/lib/liblex.a`.

#### Step 2: Re-pack the archive (Zig master workaround)

Zig master produces archives with non-8-byte-aligned members that Apple's linker rejects. Re-pack with the system `ar`:

```sh
cd zig-out/lib && \
  tmp=$(mktemp -d) && \
  (cd "$tmp" && ar x "$(cd - && pwd)/liblex.a" && chmod 644 *.o && /usr/bin/ar rcs "$(cd - && pwd)/liblex.a" *.o) && \
  rm -rf "$tmp" && \
  cd ../..
```

#### Step 3: Compile Swift and link with the Zig library

```sh
swiftc macos/Lex.swift \
    -import-objc-header src/liblex.h \
    -Lzig-out/lib -llex \
    -o macos/Lex.app/Contents/MacOS/Lex
```

#### Step 4: Sign the app (ad-hoc, for local development)

```sh
codesign -f -s - macos/Lex.app
```

</details>

## Signing

### Ad-hoc (local development)

Ad-hoc signing is sufficient for local testing and is performed automatically by `zig build`:

```sh
codesign -f -s - macos/Lex.app
```

### Hardened Runtime (for notarization)

Requires a signing identity and entitlements:

```sh
codesign -f -s "YOUR_IDENTITY" \
    --options runtime \
    macos/Lex.app
```

#### Finding your signing identity

List available identities:

```sh
security find-identity -v -p codesigning
```

## Permissions

**Accessibility** is required for `CGEventTap` to intercept keyboard events. This is a TCC (Transparency, Consent, and Control) permission — not an entitlement.

On first launch, the app calls `AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions()` which prompts the user. Grant access in:

> **System Settings → Privacy & Security → Accessibility**

Then relaunch the app.

## Known Limitations

This is a **prototype-grade** application:

- **Synthetic event fragility** — the backspace+multi-char-insert approach is unreliable in terminal emulators, Electron-based apps, and secure input fields where `CGEventTap` may be blocked or events reordered. Some apps may not handle multi-character `keyboardSetUnicodeString` correctly.
- **Single-char assumption** — the engine expects one text-producing `UniChar` per `keyDown` event, which may fail for complex dead-key layouts or surrogate pairs.
- **`ươ` auto-fill edge cases** — the auto-fill heuristic for `ươ` only triggers when a coda consonant is present and may not cover all valid Vietnamese syllable patterns.
- **Secure input fields block the tap** — password fields and other secure text inputs disable event taps by design.
- **Non-US layouts not handled** — only tested with standard US keyboard layout.
- **No Dock icon** — `LSUIElement=true` means the app only appears in the menubar.

Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Introduction to Vietnamese typing > The 5 accent marks
Content:
acute accent(“´”), grave(“`”), hook(” ̉ “), tilde(“~”) and dot(“.”)
After a closer look, you would probably agree that what you need to know how to type are just *ă* , *â* , *ê* , *ô* , *ơ* , *ư* and the 5 diacritics.
In order to type those “foreign” symbols using the standard English keyboard, there are many different typing conventions, or formally “input methods”. Nowadays, two of the most widely used conventions are the Telex and the Vni. The main difference between these two conventions is that Telex uses letters (a-z) while Vni uses numbers (0-9) to express the accent marks and the marks on top of the vowels (i.e., to type *ă* , *â* , *ê* , *ô* , *ơ* , *ư* )
 ... 
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Installing Vietnamese keyboard
Content:
In the Telex Convention, for example, you’ll type the sequence “aa” (two a’s) to get *â* . Please go ahead and try typing *aa* on your favorite text editor to see what you get. What did you get?
Of course, it’s … *aa* !
This is because by default, your computer has yet to support Vietnamese typing, which means that you need to either install a software or enable Vietnamese typing if it’s already shipped with your Operating System (for example, on Mac and Gnu/Linux). Once that piece of software has been properly installed and the mode to type Vietnamese is selected, you’ll get *â* whenever you type the sequence “aa”. Please refer to the Notes (*) below for more details on the various Vietnamese typing software.
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Installing Vietnamese keyboard
Content:
To practice typing Vietnamese online using Telex or Vni typing methods, as you’d be shown below, you can use this [no-frill Vietnamese typepad](http://realedu.opalstacked.com/learn-vietnamese/type-vietnamese-online-unikey-vni/) or using [the typepad on this site](https://vntyping.com/) .
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Type Vietnamese using Telex > Typing *â* , *ê* , *ô*
Content:
As you can observe, these 3 letters all have the circumflex. The rule to type them is hence the same:
Telex: Type the underlying letter twice. For example, to get *â* , your typing sequence is “aa”. Similarly, “oo” for *ô* and “ee” for *ê* .
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Type Vietnamese using Telex > Typing *ư* , *ơ* and *ă*
Content:
These first 2 letters share the “horn”; the last one is not quite the same but is close enough, at least according to the Telex way of typing.
Telex: type the underlying letter + *w* . For exaple, to get *ư* , your typing sequence is “uw”. Similarly, “ow” for *ơ* and “aw” for *ă* .
 ... 
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Type Vietnamese using Telex > Typing the 5 diacritics
Content:
| Diacritics | acute accent(“´”) | grave(“`”) | hook(” ̉ “) | tilde(“~”) | dot(“.”) |
| Telex | s | f | r | x | j |
| Example with a | á = as | à = af | ả = ar | ã = ax | ạ = aj |
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Type Vietnamese using Telex > Typing the 5 diacritics
Content:
And there you go! Congratulation on having learned how to type Vietnamese. But before we conclude, let’s do some exercises to make sure everything has been well understood. How would you type *ớ* ? Yes, it’s: *ơ* + the acute accent, so that the Telex typing sequence is “ows”.
Now, how about the word *đặng* ? If your answer is “ddawjng” for Telex, you’ve got it! The only note here is that instead of typing the dot right after the *ă* (i.e, “awj” for Telex), you can delay the dot till completing the whole word, which means your typing sequence could have been “ddawngj” for Telex. In fact many Vietnamese use this second way of typing the accent mark after completing the alphabet.
Section Title: How to type Vietnamese: a complete guide > Type Vietnamese using Telex > Typing the 5 diacritics
Content:
Now, if you have asked if it’s possible to defer the “breve” on top of the *a* till finishing the whole word, which means typing “ddajngw” instead of “ddawjng”, the answer is yes. It’s possible because the typing software, which understands our conventions, would easily figure out that your “w” is to change the *a* to *ă* . However, this type of deferral is not used by many people and is also not recommended for the reason that it may actually slow you down. Indeed, when you finish the world, you probably need to “look back” at the word and see what kind of “symbol” you need to add to the vowels. In addition, since *ă* , for example, is part of the Vietnamese Alphabet, it makes sense that we type it as one single unit.

Content:
The great news is that Mac has built-in support for Vietnamese Language. To enable Vietnamese typing support, go to *System Preferences* and open *International* . Then select the tab *Input Menu* .
For Operating System before Leopard, you would see only the *Vietnamese* option. For OS X Leopard onwards, you have the *Vietnamese Unikey* option where you can select the Telex or Vni input method as learned above!

Section Title: How to Type Vietnamese Accents: A Comprehensive Guide > ... > **Telex Method**
Content:
The **Telex** method is another popular way to type Vietnamese accents. It uses a combination of letters to represent accents:
‘s’ = dấu sắc (´)
‘f’ = dấu huyền (`)
‘r’ = dấu hỏi (?)
‘x’ = dấu ngã (~)
‘j’ = dấu nặng (.)
ă = aw

ê = ee
ô = oo
ơ – ow
ư = uw

For example:
To type “chào”, type “chafo”
To type “đẹp”, type “ddejp”
Both VNI and Telex are easy to set up and can be used effectively once you get the hang of their respective methods. They are also widely supported on Vietnamese typing software such as UNIKey.

Section Title: How to Type Vietnamese Accents: A Comprehensive Guide > ... > **iOS (iPhone/iPad)**
Content:
Go to **Settings** > **General** > **Keyboard** > **Keyboards** .
Add the **Vietnamese** keyboard.
Once the keyboard is added, you can choose either the **Telex** or **VNI** input method.
When typing, tap and hold the **world icon** (????) located at the bottom left of the keyboard to switch to the Vietnamese keyboard.
Type your word as you normally would using the chosen input method (Telex or VNI)
Section Title: How to Type Vietnamese Accents: A Comprehensive Guide > ... > **Android**
Content:
Go to Settings > General Management > Language and Input > On-screen Keyboard (or “Virtual Keyboard” on some devices) > Samsung Keyboard.
Tap “Language and Types” or “Select Input Languages” depending on the device and then “MANAGE INPUT LANGUAGES” if available.
Select “Tiếng Việt” from the list. Then, you can choose either the **“Telex Qwerty”** or “ **VNI Qwerty”** input method.
If you do not see “Tiếng Việt” listed, select “Manage Input Languages”, search and download Tiếng Việt in the available languages section.
Once you have downloaded, activate Vietnamese by turning the switch next to Tiếng Việt on.
When typing, press and hold the space bar on the keyboard and slide it to the language that you want to select.
Typing Vietnamese on smartphones is intuitive, as most keyboards now support diacritical marks and accent selection. It is ideal for both casual texting and more formal writing.
Section Title: ... > Tips for Typing Vietnamese Using the Telex Method
Content:
Telex is the most popular typing method for texting because it is the **fastest typing method** on mobile phones. The reason for this is that the keys to add accents are located right around the main letter keys, so you don’t have to move your finger far to type the accents. This makes typing with Telex especially quick and efficient when you’re writing in Vietnamese.
However, when you need to type in **Vietnamese** and another language, such as **English** , Telex can sometimes add **unwanted accents** to letters. This happens because Telex is designed to add accents to characters like vowels, and it might mistakenly place an accent on an English letter.
If you’re typing multiple languages together and don’t want the unwanted accents, **VNI** may be a better choice as it is less likely to interfere with non-Vietnamese characters. But if you prefer Telex, there are simple ways to fix these issues:
Section Title: ... > Tips for Typing Vietnamese Using the Telex Method
Content:
**Switching to another language** : You can quickly switch between Vietnamese and other language keyboards by tapping the **world icon** (????) on your keyboard. This will allow you to type in English or other languages without adding Vietnamese accents to non-Vietnamese words.
**Double-tapping letters** : Another quick fix when Telex is activated is to type the letter **twice** if it becomes accented. For example, if you type “of,” it will turn into “ò.” To avoid this, just type “f” again, and it will return to “of.” So, to type **“off”** when Telex is on, simply type **“off”** — the second “f” will override the accent.
With these tips, you can easily switch between languages and ensure your typing experience is smooth and efficient, whether you’re using Telex or VNI.

input.context: I need a design-level analysis only, no implementation. Focus on standard Vietnamese Telex behavior for the `a` key (`aa -> â`, `aw -> ă`, repeated-key literal/cancellation behavior), plus this repo's custom direction: non-Vietnamese onset letters can disable Vietnamese processing, `literal_index`/literal mode exists, and the prototype engine uses a cancellation rule where repeating an already-applied modifier strips it and appends a plain letter to recover literal text. Please identify missing behavior cases, edge cases, and any design risks in limiting the `'A','a'` branch to immediate-last-span logic versus broader word-level targeting. Avoid all code snippets and pseudo-code.

run.result:
1) TL;DR

For the `'A'/'a'` branch, the simple and correct design is: keep `aa -> â` as a strictly local, end-of-word action on the immediate last active `a/A`, but gate it with word-level eligibility checks. In other words, use word-level context only to decide whether Vietnamese processing is allowed at all; do not use broader word-level search to find some earlier `a` to modify.

2) Recommended approach (simple path)

Scope: M (1–3h) once implemented, mostly in branch logic + tests.

1. Define the `'A'/'a'` branch as a small set of explicit outcomes

   The branch should cover these cases clearly:

   - Literal bypass:
     - If the engine is already in literal mode, or the current append position is at/after `literal_index`, the key is appended as plain `a/A`.
     - No Vietnamese reinterpretation should happen in this state.

   - Onset-disabled word:
     - If the current word began with a non-Vietnamese onset letter under this repo’s custom rule (`f`, `j`, `w`, `z`), then `'a'` must stay literal for the rest of that word.
     - This includes blocking `aa -> â`.

   - Standard apply:
     - If the immediate last active span is plain `a/A`, then `'a'` applies circumflex to that span.
     - This should also work if that `a/A` already carries a tone; the tone should remain on the same span.

   - Standard cancellation / literal recovery:
     - If the immediate last active span is already `â/Â`, another `'a'` should remove that circumflex, clear the tone on that span as per the prototype rule, and append a plain `a/A`.
     - This matches the stated recovery model for literal text.

   - Everything else:
     - Append plain `a/A`.
     - Do not search backward through the word for another `a` to modify.

2. Keep `'aa'` target selection local, not word-level

   For standard Telex, doubled-vowel modifiers like `aa`, `ee`, `oo` are adjacency-driven. They are not like tone keys, and they are not like the broader `w` behavior that may need vowel targeting.

   So for the `a` key:
   - local target selection is correct,
   - broader retroactive search is not.

   This is the main design recommendation.

3. Use word-level context only as a gate, not as a target finder

   The branch still needs some broader context, but only for yes/no decisions:

   - Is the current word Telex-eligible, or disabled by a non-Vietnamese onset?
   - Is the current position already in literal suffix territory because of `literal_index`?
   - Is the active editable area still inside the effective buffer?

   That context should decide whether processing is allowed. It should not decide which earlier `a` to mutate.

4. Make case handling explicit

   The branch should be case-insensitive for recognition, but case-preserving in output:

   - `aA`, `Aa`, `AA`, `aa` should all be treated as the same modifier intent.
   - The modified character should preserve the case of the target span.
   - If cancellation appends a literal letter, that appended letter should preserve the case of the actual key pressed.

5. Treat cross-modifier situations as non-convertible

   If the last active span is already `ă/Ă`, then pressing `a/A` should not attempt to convert it into `â/Â`.
   Likewise, if the last active span is some other vowel or a consonant, the key should remain a plain appended `a/A`.

   This avoids hidden modifier replacement rules that users will not expect.

6. Add tests around the missing behavior matrix

   The branch is missing clear behavioral coverage for:

   - plain `aa -> â`
   - case variants: `aa`, `aA`, `Aa`, `AA`
   - `â + a -> aa` recovery
   - `ấ + a -> aa` recovery with tone clearing
   - `á + a -> ấ` style “apply circumflex to already-toned a”
   - `aw + a` staying as `ă` plus literal `a`
   - triple and repeated sequences like `aaa`, `aaaa`
   - onset-disabled words such as words beginning with `f/j/w/z`
   - behavior when `literal_index` is already set
   - correct modification tracking for mutate-only vs mutate-and-append outcomes

3) Rationale and trade-offs

Why this is the right simple model:

- It matches standard Telex expectations for doubled-vowel modifiers.
- It stays consistent with the prototype’s cancellation rule.
- It avoids surprising retroactive edits to older letters in the word.
- It fits the repo’s custom needs: onset-disable and literal suffix behavior.

Why broader word-level targeting is unnecessary now:

- It is not standard for `aa`.
- It creates ambiguity when there are multiple `a` letters in the current word.
- It interacts badly with `literal_index`, because a later key could reach back across a boundary that was supposed to freeze processing.
- It makes cancellation less predictable: the user presses `a` at the end, but some older `a` changes instead.

So the clean split is:

- word-level context for eligibility,
- immediate-last-span logic for actual `aa` targeting.

4) Risks and guardrails

1. Biggest correctness risk: immediate-last-span without onset gating

   If the branch only looks at the last span and ignores word onset, it will violate the repo’s custom rule. A word that starts with `f/j/w/z` could still accidentally produce `â`.

   Guardrail: treat onset-disable as a word-level veto before any `aa` logic.

2. Biggest design risk with broader targeting: nonlocal edits

   If the branch scans backward for “some eligible `a`,” users may get edits to earlier letters that they did not just type against. That is especially risky in words with multiple vowels or after cancellation/literal recovery.

   Guardrail: do not broaden the target search for the `a` key.

3. Literal boundary risk

   Once `literal_index` exists, broader search can accidentally mutate spans that are supposed to be on the frozen side of the boundary.

   Guardrail: the `a` branch should only ever affect the immediate active tail, never reach across `literal_index`.

4. Destructive cancellation is irreversible without history

   Under the prototype rule, cancelling `â` removes its modifier and tone, then appends a plain `a`. That is simple and valid, but destructive. If the user backspaces afterward, they will not automatically get the original `â` or `ấ` back unless the engine stores history.

   Guardrail: accept this as the current behavior and document/test it. Do not accidentally imply reversibility.

5. Mixed-case ambiguity

   If case behavior is not defined, cross-case sequences will feel inconsistent.

   Guardrail: define matching as case-insensitive, output as case-preserving.

6. Interaction asymmetry with `w`

   `aw -> ă` belongs to the `w` rule, but the `a` branch still needs to respect that result. If `a` starts replacing an existing breve, the modifier model becomes inconsistent.

   Guardrail: different modifiers should not replace each other implicitly.

5) When to consider the advanced path

Only revisit this if one of these becomes a real requirement:

- Users explicitly want to type the extra `a` later in the syllable, not immediately after the target `a`.
- You want more permissive, less-standard Telex behavior for vowel-shape keys.
- You want backspace after cancellation to restore the previous composed state.
- The onset-disable rule grows into a fuller syllable parser with richer word analysis.
- You find many real words where local `aa` handling is insufficient and users complain.

6) Optional advanced path

If you later need more flexibility, the next step is not “search the whole word for any `a`.” The safer advanced path is:

- track a small per-word composition region,
- distinguish active Vietnamese-editable prefix from literal suffix,
- allow limited retargeting only inside the active nucleus or tail,
- optionally store reversible edit history for cancellation/backspace behavior.

But that is extra complexity, and nothing in your current requirements justifies it for the `'A'/'a'` branch.

Bottom line: for `'A'/'a'`, keep the actual modifier action immediate-last-span only, but wrap it in word-level guards for onset disable and literal boundaries. That gives you standard Telex behavior, matches the prototype’s cancellation model, and avoids the main design traps.

### Proposed Handling Model for 'A'/'a' Branch in `lex_add` 

Based on the `liblex.zig` prototype and development logs, the handling of the 'A'/'a' branch follows these specific rules and assumptions:

#### 1. Core Telex Transformations
- **`aa` → `â` (Circumflex):** If the input is 'a' and the preceding character is also an 'a' (without a modifier), the preceding 'a' is updated with the `circumflex` diacritic.
- **`aw` → `ă` (Breve):** While 'w' is the primary trigger for the breve diacritic, the 'a' branch must account for how it interacts with existing 'w' modifiers in the word.

#### 2. Immediate-Last-Span vs. Word-Level Targeting
- **Current Prototype Logic:** The prototype implementation for doubled vowels (`aa`, `ee`, `oo`) currently uses **immediate-last-span logic**. It checks if `s.len > 0` and if the character at `s.len - 1` matches the current input base.
- **Broader Targeting Risk:** The Oracle was consulted to critique this approach, specifically whether the 'A'/'a' branch should look further back into the word (word-level targeting) to handle complex vowel clusters or if immediate-last-span logic is sufficient for standard Telex.

#### 3. Repeated-Key Cancellation and Literal Recovery
- **Cancellation Rule:** If a modifier key is pressed when the target character already has that specific modifier applied, the engine strips the modifier and the tone, then appends a plain version of the letter.
- **Example:** If the state is `â` (base 'a', modifier 'a') and the user presses 'a' again, the engine: 
    1. Resets the existing `â` to a plain `a` (modifier = 0, tone = 0).
    2. Appends a new plain `a` to the buffer.
- **Goal:** This allows users to recover literal text (e.g., typing "aa" to get "aa" instead of "â") by repeating the key.

#### 4. Interaction with `literal_index` and Flags
- **Literal Mode:** The engine uses a `mode` (0 for NORMAL, 1 for LITERAL) and a `flags` bitfield on the `Span` struct.
- **`is_literal` Flag:** Bit 1 of the `flags` byte marks a span as literal, meaning it should be rendered as its raw ASCII base character regardless of Telex rules. The `lex_add` function must respect these flags to prevent double-processing.

#### 5. Non-Vietnamese Onset Disabling
- **Rule:** If a word starts with a non-Vietnamese consonant (**f, j, w, z**), Vietnamese processing (diacritics and tones) should be disabled for the remainder of that word.
- **Implementation:** The `lex_add` logic must check the onset of the current word state before applying the 'a' -> 'â' transformation.

#### 6. Identified Risks and Open Questions
- **Targeting Ambiguity:** There is a design concern regarding whether the 'a' key should only modify the *immediately* preceding 'a' or if it should search for the "correct" target vowel in a nucleus (e.g., in a triphthong).
- **Tone Clearing:** The current model assumes that applying or canceling a diacritic should clear the tone on that vowel to avoid invalid combinations, but the exact sequence of tone re-application is a point of analysis.
- **Buffer Management:** The engine must handle `literal_index` to determine which parts of the current word are still "active" for Telex composition versus which have been finalized as literal text.

1	# Prototype — Span-based Vietnamese Telex IME
     2	
     3	A playground prototype for a Vietnamese Telex input method on macOS. The core engine (`liblex`) is a Zig static library that models each rendered character as a **span** (base + modifier + tone), handling the full Telex input set: all vowels with diacritics (â, ă, ê, ô, ơ, ư), tones (sắc, huyền, hỏi, ngã, nặng), đ, cancellation logic, `gi`/`qu` onset reclassification, and `ươ` auto-fill. The shell (`Lex.swift`) is a macOS menubar app that intercepts keystrokes via `CGEventTap`, feeds them to the engine, and applies a **diff** against the currently rendered text — emitting only the minimal sequence of synthetic backspaces followed by a single multi-character insertion event to update the active application.
     4	
     5	## Architecture
     6	
     7	The system is split into two layers communicating through a C ABI:
     8	
     9	- **Core (`src/liblex.zig`)** — A span-based composition engine. Each keystroke mutates an array of `Span` structs (base letter, modifier, tone, flags). The engine handles modifier application (doubled vowels `aa`→`â`, `w`→`ơ`/`ư`/`ă`, `dd`→`đ`), tone placement following Vietnamese linguistic rules (region analysis: onset → nucleus → coda, with semi-vowel coda detection), cancellation (repeating a filled modifier/tone strips it and enters literal mode), `gi`/`qu` onset binding, and `ươ` auto-fill. Unicode output is produced via lookup tables covering all 12 vowel bases × 6 tone variants in both cases. The engine exposes `add`, `backspace`, `reset`, `get_composed_utf16`, `init_state`, and `state_size`.
    10	
    11	- **Shell (`macos/Lex.swift`)** — A menubar-only macOS app (`LSUIElement=true`) that owns the engine state and a rendered-text snapshot. On each keystroke it calls the engine, reads the new composed UTF-16, and diffs it against the snapshot to compute the minimal edit (common-prefix comparison). Replacement characters are emitted in a single multi-character `CGEvent` to reduce flicker. Synthetic events are tagged (`0x4C4558`) to avoid feedback loops. The shell resets engine state on app switches, mouse clicks, modifier keys (Cmd/Ctrl/Alt), navigation keys (arrows, Home/End, Tab, Escape, Return, forward delete), and tap-disabled events. A global hotkey (Ctrl+Opt+Space) toggles Vietnamese input on/off with audio feedback.
    12	
    13	- **FFI bridge (`src/liblex.h`)** — C-compatible header declaring the engine API. Swift calls into the Zig static library through this bridging header. The engine is stateless from the caller's perspective — Swift allocates the opaque state buffer (`state_size()` bytes) and passes it to every call.
    14	
    15	## Prerequisites
    16	
    17	- **Zig ≥ 0.16.0** (master recommended) — [download](https://ziglang.org/download/)
    18	- **Xcode Command Line Tools** — `xcode-select --install`
    19	- **macOS 26** (Tahoe)
    20	
    21	## Project Structure
    22	
    23	```
    24	prototype-app/
    25	├── src/
    26	│   ├── liblex.zig              # Span-based Telex engine (C library)
    27	│   └── liblex.h                # C bridging header
    28	├── macos/
    29	│   ├── Lex.app/
    30	│   │   └── Contents/

18	- macOS 26.0 trở về sau.
    19	
    20	## Chức năng
    21	
    22	- [ ] Nhập Tiếng Việt kiểu Telex với một số tùy chỉnh:
    23	  - [ ] Bỏ dấu kiểu cũ: _òa_, _úy_. Ví dụ: _hòa_, _thúy_.
    24	  - [ ] Không bỏ dấu nếu từ bắt đầu bằng phụ âm không phải Tiếng Việt: _f_, _j_, _w_, _z_.
    25	- [ ] Phím tắt bật / tắt nhập Tiếng Việt: `Ctrl + Opt + Space`.

In the current [`'A', 'a'` branch](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L248-L270), the comment list is too small. Under Telex, the branch is not just “circumflex / cancel circumflex / literal”.

## Missing Cases

- Plain append in Telex mode: first `a`, or any `a` that does not have an immediately eligible target, should append a plain `a` while staying in Telex mode. That is different from full literal bypass.
- Apply circumflex to a plain trailing `a/A`: `aa -> â`, case-insensitive in recognition, but preserving the target span’s case.
- Apply circumflex to an already toned `a/A`: if the current last active span is `á`, `à`, `ả`, `ã`, or `ạ`, another `a` should produce the circumflex form with the same tone, not drop the tone.
- Cancel a previously applied circumflex: if the last active span is `â/Â`, another `a` should recover literal text. The standard Telex recovery rule is “repeat the second key to get distinct characters”, so this is the `aaa -> aa` family of behavior.
- Cancel a toned circumflex: if the last active span is `ấ/ầ/ẩ/ẫ/ậ`, recovery must also define what happens to the tone. The prototype chooses to clear tone on the modified span, then append a plain `a`.
- Cross-modifier refusal: if the last active span is `ă/Ă`, pressing `a` should not silently replace breve with circumflex. It should append plain `a`.
- Non-local fallback: if the nearest preceding `a` is not the immediate active tail, `a` should not scan backward through the word to find some older `a` to modify.
- Custom onset gate: the repo explicitly wants words beginning with non-Vietnamese onset letters `f`, `j`, `w`, `z` to stay unaccented, so `a` must be blocked from `aa -> â` inside those words too, not just handled locally. That requirement is stated in the main [README](file:///<redacted>/lex/README.md#L22-L24).
- Literal-boundary gate: the state comments define `literal_index` as a position boundary, not a global permanent flag, so the branch conceptually needs “current position is at/after literal boundary” as a case distinct from “some literal index exists somewhere”. The current condition in [the branch](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L249-L263) is broader than that design comment suggests.

## Approach

The clean approach is:

- Treat `a` as a local doubled-vowel modifier, like the prototype does in its [doubled-vowel block](file:///<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/src/liblex.zig#L446-L465).
- Use word-level context only as a gate, not as a target finder. The gate is for things like the custom non-Vietnamese onset rule and literal-boundary behavior.
- Keep target selection immediate-tail only. For `a`, that means:
  - if the last active span is plain `a/A`, apply circumflex;
  - if the last active span is already circumflexed `a/A`, cancel into literal recovery;
  - otherwise append plain `a/A`.
- Preserve tone when applying circumflex to an already toned `a`.
- Do not let `a` replace a different diacritic already on `a`, especially breve. `aw` belongs to the `w` rule, so the `a` rule should respect that separation.
- Make state transitions explicit, because this branch has three different kinds of outcomes:
  - modify existing span only;
  - modify existing span and append one new plain `a`;
  - append one plain `a` with no modification.
- Define case handling explicitly: recognition should be case-insensitive, but output should preserve the target span’s case for modification and the pressed key’s case for any appended recovery character.

One deeper design issue sits underneath this: if `buffer_length` is meant to track the effective visible spans, then `aa -> â` is a modify-only case and should not grow visible length. If it is meant to track raw keypress count, the current state shape is missing a separate effective-span length. The comments around [`State`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L194-L218) read more like effective-buffer semantics than raw-key history, so this branch should be designed around visible-span updates.

## Oracle Perspective

Oracle agreed with the main direction and pushed one point hard: for `a`, broader word-level search is the wrong model.

- Use broader context only to decide whether Vietnamese processing is allowed at all.
- Do not search backward through the word for an older `a` to mutate.
- Keep `aa` strictly local to the immediate last active `a`, because that matches standard Telex better and avoids surprising retroactive edits.
- The main risks are:
  - forgetting the repo’s onset-disable rule;
  - letting `a` reach across a literal boundary;
  - silently replacing `ă` with `â`;
  - leaving mixed-case behavior undefined;
  - not testing repeated sequences like `aaa` and `aaaa`.

The prototype architecture description in [the prototype README](file:///<redacted>/lex/playground/prototype-app/README.md#L3-L10) is consistent with that: doubled-vowel modifiers are local, while broader syllable analysis is reserved for tone placement and other word-level features.
